question_title: Will an "enmienda a la totalidad" against Spain's AI governance bill (LOIA) be published before July 24, 2026?
summary: The question requires an enmienda a la totalidad to be registered and published in the BOCG specifically between July 11-23, 2026. The question description explicitly states the amendment deadline was extended to September 2, 2026. In Spanish parliamentary procedure, amendments to the entirety are typically published in the BOCG after the deadline closes, and groups usually file near the deadline. August recess further disincentivizes early-mid July action. Research confirms no such amendment has been published as of mid-July, and this absence is procedurally normal with the window still open. Even though opposition groups have criticized the bill and may eventually file, the specific timing in this narrow window is very unlikely. The premortem flagged that IF the deadline were actually near the window, publication would be likely — but the question text itself establishes the September extension, so I keep a low but not negligible probability to cover residual scenario uncertainty and the possibility of an early low-effort 'de devolución' filing for political effect.

factors.description: Amendments to the entirety are typically published in BOCG only after the amendment deadline closes; the question states the deadline was extended to 2 September 2026, so publication in the July 11-23 window is procedurally unusual
Groups tend to file amendments near the deadline; with the deadline pushed to Sept 2 and August recess intervening, strong incentive to wait until late August/early September
As of mid-July 2026, no enmienda a la totalidad registered or published (confirmed by research)
Minority coalition and several critical groups make an eventual enmienda a la totalidad plausible; a low-effort 'de devolución' could be filed early for political/media effect

reasoning:
**Forecast: 5.5% (NO likely)**

The question asks whether an "enmienda a la totalidad" against Spain's LOIA bill (expediente 121/000096) will be registered and published in the BOCG specifically between July 11-23, 2026.

**Key reasoning:**
- The amendment-submission deadline was extended to **September 2, 2026** (per the question's own authoritative procedural description).
- In Spanish parliamentary procedure, amendments to the entirety are typically published in the BOCG **after** the deadline closes, and groups usually file near the deadline.
- The intervening **August recess** further pushes activity to late August/early September.
- Research confirmed **no such amendment has been published** as of mid-July 2026, and this absence is procedurally normal with the window still open.

The main risk (flagged in premortem) is that an opposition group could file a low-effort "de devolución" motion early for political effect — which is why I hold a small but non-negligible 5.5% rather than dismissing it entirely. The status quo strongly favors NO.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between July 11, 2026 and 23:59 Madrid time on July 23, 2026, at least one "enmienda a la totalidad" (a motion either to return the bill to the Government — "de devolución" — or proposing a complete alternative text — "con texto alternativo") against the Proyecto de Ley Orgánica para el buen uso y la gobernanza de la inteligencia artificial (Congreso de los Diputados expediente número 121/000096) is registered by a parliamentary group and published in the Boletín Oficial de las Cortes Generales (BOCG). The resolution can be checked via the official Congreso publications search (https://www.congreso.es/es/busqueda-de-publicaciones), or via [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). If no such amendment to the entirety has been published by that time, the question resolves NO.
fine_print: Only an amendment formally classified as an "enmienda a la totalidad" (whether "de devolución" or "de texto alternativo") counts, in whichever BOCG series it is published. Partial amendments to specific articles ("enmiendas al articulado") do not count. External amendment proposals sent to parliamentary groups by associations, lobbyists, or other non-parliamentary actors do not count; the amendment must be one formally registered by a parliamentary group and appearing in the BOCG. If the amendment-submission period is further extended and no amendment to the entirety is published by the deadline, the question resolves NO.

factors.description: Amendments to the entirety are typically published in BOCG only AFTER the amendment deadline closes; the deadline was extended to 2 September 2026, so publication in the July 11-23 window is procedurally unusual
Groups tend to file amendments near the deadline; with deadline pushed to Sept 2 and August recess intervening, there is strong incentive to wait until late August/early September
As of mid-July 2026, no enmienda a la totalidad has been registered or published (confirmed by research)
Minority coalition and several groups have voiced criticism, so eventual filing of an enmienda a la totalidad is plausible (but timing is the key issue, not whether it happens eventually)

input.anchor: Base rate for a specific parliamentary amendment being filed AND published in a narrow 13-day window ~7 weeks before an extended deadline: low, ~5-8%
input.assessment: The question requires an enmienda a la totalidad to be registered AND published in BOCG in the narrow July 11-23 window. The amendment deadline was extended to Sept 2, 2026. In Spanish procedure, amendments to the entirety are usually published in the BOCG after the deadline closes, and groups typically wait until near the deadline to file. August recess further disincentivizes early-mid July action. Even if opposition groups eventually file, the specific timing in this 13-day window is very unlikely. Status quo strongly resolves NO.
input.calibration_notes: Status quo overwhelmingly favors NO. Not hedging toward 50% — evidence genuinely points to a low probability. Deadline extension is the dominant factor pushing activity to September.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether a group makes an early political statement by filing an enmienda a la totalidad well before the deadline (possible but uncommon), and whether BOCG would publish it promptly rather than batching after deadline.
input.tool_audit: research() returned useful confirmation that no enmienda has been published as of mid-July and that absence is procedurally normal with the window still open. Official Congreso page returned HTTP 403, so live confirmation of exact deadline extension rests on question context, which is consistent.
input.process_reflection: Straightforward status-quo timing question. The research tool was sufficient; no need for simulation. The main friction was the 403 on the official Congreso page, but the question description itself provides the authoritative procedural context.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
SUMMARY: The forecast direction (NO likely) is defensible, but the two dominant negative factors rest on an UNVERIFIED premise the agent injected itself, and the agent never queried the authoritative source the resolution criteria names. This warrants a warn, and I'd nudge the probability up from 4.5%.

FINDING 1 — Unverified premise driving the dominant factors (the "2 September 2026 extension").
Factors 1 (logit −2.5) and 2 (logit −1.5) — together ~−4.0 of the ~−4.5 total logit — both hinge on the claim that the amendment deadline "was extended to 2 September 2026." I traced this date's provenance:
- It first appears in the agent's OWN research query context (trace lines 16 and 38: "Amendment deadline was extended to 2 September 2026").
- The research sub-agent then echoes it back but EXPLICITLY flags it as unverified: "the '2 September 2026' date rests on your provided context plus consistency with the original deadline, rather than an independently retrieved published record" (line 57). The worldview research entry (spain-loia...fbc8f810.json) repeats the same caveat.
- The ONLY deadline verified by actual sources (ControlCongreso, Novatierra press) is 30 June 2026 at 18:00.
- The question's resolution criteria contain NO September 2 date. The fine print only says "If the amendment-submission period is further extended... resolves NO" — implying an extension may exist but giving no date.

So the September 2 date is neither in the question nor confirmed by any retrieved source; it is an assumption the agent fed into its own research and then treated as established fact in Factor 1 with confidence 0.85 ("the deadline was extended to 2 September 2026"). This is not a clean hallucination (the agent's research honestly flagged the gap), but the FACTORS present it without the caveat, which overstates the evidentiary basis of the core negative case.

FINDING 2 — Forecast direction is regime-dependent.
The whole negative case flips under the alternative scenario. If the real deadline were 30 June (the only verified date) or any date falling within/just before the July 11–23 window, then enmiendas a la totalidad would be published in BOCG PRECISELY around now (they publish right after the deadline closes) — pushing the probability substantially higher, plausibly toward/above 50%. The forecast's low probability is entirely contingent on the window still being open past July 23, which the agent assumed rather than verified.

Mitigating factor that keeps this at warn, not fail: the question was published 2026-07-13 with fine print explicitly anticipating "if the period is further extended... resolves NO." An author writing that fine print strongly implies the window is still open as of publication (otherwise the clause is moot). That is indirect but real corroboration for the agent's direction. Combined with the genuinely low base rate for a specific parliamentary filing AND BOCG publication within a narrow 13-day window, NO remains the more likely outcome.

FINDING 3 — Authoritative source never queried.
The resolution criteria name a specific checkable source: the Congreso publications search (busqueda-de-publicaciones). The agent hit the tramitación page (403) and ControlCongreso (a stale 12/06 snapshot), but NEVER queried the BOCG publications search — the single most decisive endpoint for confirming whether an enmienda a la totalidad was actually published. Given the 403 on the primary page, this was the natural fallback and it was skipped.

PROBABILITY ASSESSMENT: I put the probability around 8–12%, higher than the agent's 4.5%. The agent double-discounts nothing but instead UNDER-weights the scenario risk that the deadline falls within/near the window (which would make publication likely), and it anchors too tightly to an unverified extension. The 4.5% and the stated confidence of 0.85 are inconsistent with the fact that the entire negative case would invert under a plausible alternative reading of the (unverified) procedural status. A calibrated estimate in the high-single-digits to low-teens better reflects this.

OTHER CHECKS: Factor signs are all correctly directed. Factor 4 (+0.5) correctly captures that eventual filing is plausible but timing is the issue — no wrong-direction problem. No double-counting beyond the natural relatedness of factors 1/2 (both procedural-timing, both leaning on the same extension premise — this is close to double-counting the same unverified fact, adding to the overconfidence concern). Tool audit is honest (acknowledges the 403). The counterargument is genuinely engaged (it constructs the "low-effort devolución filed early for media attention, BOCG publishes on rolling basis" alternative). Self-confidence of 0.85 is the main calibration miss given the unverified linchpin.
